preamble: The shift from the palato-alveolar affricate [tʃ] back to the velar stop [k] is a rare process known as depalatalization or revelarization. Because the natural direction of sound change is from velars to palatals (palatalization), the reverse usually occurs only through specific external pressures like dialect contact, loanword influence, or hypercorrection.

examples.note: OE 'cirice' [tʃ] survived in Standard English 'church'; 'kirk' reflects the competing Old Norse form 'kirkja' [k], which never underwent OE palatalization. Strictly a Norse borrowing rather than depalatalization.

sources: Baugh, A. C. & Cable, T. (2013). A History of the English Language (6th ed.). Routledge.
